krama: नमो॒ नील॑ग्रीवाय । नील॑ग्रीवाय च । च शिति॒कण्ठा॑य । शिति॒कण्ठा॑य च । च इति॑ च ॥

sayana.english: 'Nilagrivah' is he who has a part of his neck blue in color due to holding the Kalakuta poison. 'Shitikanthah' is he who has the remaining neck region white (shiti).

dhyana.english: May one meditate on Hara, who has three eyes, is seated on a bull, accompanied by the lord of hosts (Ganesha), and whose entire body is smeared with ashes and adorned with a crown of matted hair.

bhatta_bhaskara.english:
'Namo nilagrivaya cha'—explained already. 'Shitikanthaya cha'—because 'shiti' means both white and dark according to the Nighantu. Indeed, only a small part of the God's neck became blue due to the fire of the Kalakuta poison. Or: the repetition is for the sake of respect, as in "Salutation to the Lord of Ambika, the Lord of Uma, the Lord of Beings." [Grammatical note on the word 'shitikanthaya']. (3)

Salutation to the one with matted hair and to the one with shaven hair.

dhyana.english: May one meditate on Hara, who has three eyes, is seated on a bull, accompanied by the lord of hosts (Ganesha), and whose entire body is smeared with ashes and adorned with a crown of matted hair.

abhinava_shankara.sanskrit:
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं मोक्षहेतुत्वमुक्तम् । श्रृणावि हिनस्ति सर्वमिति
शर्वः, अनेन 'तस्मा उपसंहर्तें महामासाय नमः' इत्यादि-
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्संहर्तृत्वमुक्तम् । पशुपतिशब्देन बन्धस्थि-
तिहेतुत्वमुच्यते, पशुशब्देन संसारबन्धस्य पतिशब्देन तद्र-
क्षणस्य भोक्तेः ; तथा च 'संसारमोक्षस्थितिबन्धहेतुः' इति
श्रुतिप्रसिद्धं जगत्स्थितिबन्धहेतुत्वमुक्तं भवतीत्याह्य पशुकु-
क्षहेतुत्वेन भगवन्नमस्कारो मन्त्रद्वयार्थः । अत्र नमःशब्द-
ल प्रत्येकं चतुष्पर्यन्तपदाभ्यामन्वयं दर्शियितुं समुच्चयार्थौ च-
कारौ फलितौ । तेन भवाय च नमः शर्वाय च नमः इत्य-
न्वयो भवति । एवममेऽपि सर्वत्र द्रष्टव्यम् ॥

      नमो नीलग्रीवाय च शितिकण्ठाय च ॥

      अथ तृतीयः:—— नमो नीलग्रीवायेति । काळकूटमसनेन
नीला ग्रीवा कण्ठो यस्य, स नीलग्रीवः, तद्वृषनात्पूर्वे
स्थितः धवलः कण्ठो यस्य, सः शितिकण्ठः । अनेन स्व-
शेत धवलकण्ठे काळकूटप्रयुक्तं नैसर्ग्य दोषमविगण्यम जगदे-
करक्षणाय विषं कबलितवतो भगवतः सर्वलोकदयालुत्वम्
'अहीमिदं दयसे विश्वम्' इत्यादिश्रुतिप्रसिद्धं प्रदशिवम् ॥

      नमः कपर्दिने च ब्युस्केशाय च ॥
abhinava_shankara.english:
The well-known Śruti passage establishes His role as the bestower of liberation (mokṣahetutva). "He who destroys (śṛṇāti hinaṣti) everything is Śarva"; through this name, the role of Śarva as the destroyer of the universe (jagat-saṃhartṛtva) is stated, as attested in the Śruti: "To that great Time who gathers all in — salutation." By the word Paśupati (lord of souls/beasts), the role of the Lord as the cause of bondage and sustenance is stated: the word paśu (animal, bound soul) refers to the bondage of saṃsāra, and the word pati (lord) refers to protection and enjoyment of that. Accordingly, the Śruti — "He is the cause of saṃsāra-liberation, sustenance, and bondage" — affirms that He is the cause of world-sustenance and bondage; hence, it is concluded that the meaning of these two mantras is homage to the Lord as the cause of the five cosmic functions relating to the herd of souls. Here, to show that the word namaḥ connects individually with each of the four sets of paired names, the conjunctive particles "ca" (and) serve to indicate coordination. Thus the connection runs: "namaḥ bhavāya ca" and "namaḥ śarvāya ca." The same should be understood for all the other pairs throughout.

      Homage to Nīlagrīva and to Śitikaṇṭha.

      Now the third [mantra]: "Namo nīlagrīvāya" — He whose throat and neck (grīvā, kaṇṭha) are blue from drinking the Kālakūṭa poison is Nīlagrīva (the blue-throated one); He whose throat was white (dhavala) before the event of the poison-drinking is Śitikaṇṭha (the white-throated one). By this mantra, the Lord's compassion for all worlds is indicated — His compassion that moved Him, disregarding the natural defect inflicted on His otherwise fair throat by the Kālakūṭa poison, to swallow the venom for the single purpose of protecting the world. This is well-attested in the Śruti: "You have compassion upon this entire world" — and similar passages.

      Homage to Kapardī and to Vyuṣkeśa.
